items.title: Cyberman HW CTF Documentation
items.text:
Welcome to Cyberman Hardware CTF!

This page serves as your complete documentation for the custom ESP32-C3 badge provided during the event. Use the CTF and Hardware Reference sections to help you hunt for flags. Once the event concludes, this board is yours to keep, and you can flash it with custom firmware.

items.title: Getting Started (Setup)
items.text:
Follow these steps to power up your Cyberman board and verify it is functioning correctly before the CTF begins.

  1. Unbox the Board: Inspect the PCB for any physical damage.
  2. Power It Up: Connect the board to your device using the provided USB Type-A to Type-C data cable.
  3. Verify Status: You should see the LEDs pulse Red, indicating the CTF is loaded. If not, then the CTF needs to be loaded.
  4. Flash the Board (OPTIONAL): Use the Web Flasher at HTTPS://WEB_FLASHER_URL to flash the board with the latest version of the CTF.

If your board does not power on, please notify a CTF admin immediately.

Click and drag to rotate the board. Scroll to zoom.

items.title: Rules & Scope
items.text:
Please read these rules carefully. Violating the scope of the CTF may result in disqualification.

  • In Scope: Extracting firmware, analyzing serial output, intercepting I2C/SPI traffic on the exposed headers, and side-channel attacks on the ESP32-C3.
  • Out of Scope: Attacking the CTF infrastructure (scoring server, this documentation site), physical destruction of the board (delidding the ESP32-C3 is not required or allowed).

Flag Format: All flags in this hardware CTF follow the standard format: cyberman{some_l33t_text_here} Submit all flags to the scoring server at: [INSERT SCORING SERVER URL]

items.title: Serial Connection Guide
items.text:
The primary way to interact with the Cyberman board during the CTF is via UART. You may use PuTTY, screen, or minicom for this.

  • Baud Rate: [115200]
  • Data Bits: 8
  • Parity: None
  • Stop Bits: 1

The ESP32-C3 features built-in USB-to-JTAG/Serial. Simply plugging the USB-C cable into your computer should expose a COM port (Windows) or /dev/ttyACM (Linux) / /dev/cu.usbmodem (macOS).

Use a terminal emulator like PuTTY, screen, or minicom: screen /dev/ttyACM0 115200

items.title: Board Overview
items.text:
The official badge and target board for the 1st Edition of the Cyberman HW CTF.

Front View

Specifications:

Resource Description SoC ESP32-C3 (Single-core 32-bit RISC-V @ 160 MHz) Wireless 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n) & Bluetooth 5 (LE) Memory 400 KB SRAM, 4 MB embedded Flash Power 5V via USB-C, regulated to 3.3V

items.title: Pinout & Interfaces
items.text:
Below is the specific GPIO routing for the Cyberman CTF board.

Board Pinout:

GPIO Board function Usage Constraints 0 – ADC1_CH0, XTAL_32K_P – 1 – ADC1_CH1, XTAL_32K_N – 2 – ADC1_CH2, BOOT Pull-up 10k 3 – ADC1_CH3 – 4 – ADC1_CH4, MTMS – 5 WS2812 LED interface ADC2_CH0, MTDI – 6 – MTCK – 7 – MTDO – 8 – LOG Pull-up 10k 9 – BOOT Pull-up 10k 10 – – 18 USB D− USB/JTAG Used for USB/JTAG, don’t use 19 USB D+ USB/JTAG Used for USB/JTAG, don’t use 20 UART0 RX Header UART0RX – 21 UART0 TX Header UART0TX –

All of the pins that can be interfaced with are PWM-capable.

Your browser does not support PDFs. Download the PDF.

items.title: 💻 Firmware
items.text: The firmware will not be released as source code, however the diagnostics will be. If you really want to dig up on the firmware, there's this page which will help you understand more about this board, and perhaps you'll uncover the last 2 secret flags.

items.title: Web Flasher
items.text:
Now that the CTF is over, the board is yours! We have provided an online Web Flasher tool so you can easily install custom, general-purpose firmware (like WLED, MicroPython, or standard AT commands) directly from your browser.

👉 Launch the Cyberman Web Flasher

Note: You must use a Web Serial compatible browser (like Chrome or Edge). Ensure your board is plugged in via USB-C, click the link above, and follow the on-screen instructions.

items.title: Firmware Extraction using esp32knife_fixed
path: ESP32-C3 Firmware Extraction and Reverse Engineering

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Clone the repository: esp32knife_fixed
  • ESP32-C3 device connected via USB
  • Python 3.x environment
path: ESP32-C3 Firmware Extraction and Reverse Engineering

items.title: Steps
items.text:
1. Install dependencies from the repository's requirements
  2. Connect your ESP32-C3 to the system
  3. Run the extraction tool following the repo's instructions to dump the firmware
  4. Extract and save the firmware binary to your working directory
path: ESP32-C3 Firmware Extraction and Reverse Engineering

items.title: Analyzing Firmware with Ghidra
path: ESP32-C3 Firmware Extraction and Reverse Engineering

items.title: Initial Setup
items.text:
• Open Ghidra and create a new project
  • Import the extracted firmware binary
  • Select correct processor architecture ?
path: ESP32-C3 Firmware Extraction and Reverse Engineering

items.title: Analysis Steps
items.text:
1. Disassemble the firmware and let Ghidra perform initial analysis
  2. Identify key functions using the Symbol Tree and Listing windows
  3. Search for strings that hint at CTF flags or sensitive data
  4. Use cross-references to trace flag-related function calls
  5. Examine memory sections for hardcoded flag data
path: ESP32-C3 Firmware Extraction and Reverse Engineering

items.title: Flag Extraction Tips
items.text:
• Look for string constants containing format patterns (CYBERMAN{...})
  • Check .rodata sections for embedded text
  • Trace cryptographic or encoding/decoding routines
path: ESP32-C3 Firmware Extraction and Reverse Engineering

items.title: MicroPython on the ESP32-C3
items.text:
This board can be flashed with MicroPython, which lets you write pythonic code that runs directly on the custom ESP32-C3 board. This guide shows how to:

  1. Flash MicroPython onto the board
  2. Connect to it from your computer
  3. Upload Python scripts
  4. Run code automatically on boot

items.title: Quick start
items.text:
pip install --user mpremote
mpremote connect auto fs ls
mpremote connect auto fs cp main.py :main.py
mpremote connect auto reset

What you need - The ESP32-C3 board - USB cable with data support - Computer with Python installed - Serial-capable USB port

items.title: 1. Flash MicroPython
items.text: If the board is already flashed with MicroPython, you can skip this    step. Flash the MicroPython firmware from the web serial or from the official website.

items.title: 2. Install the required tool
items.text:
Download mpremote, which is the standard MicroPython command-line tool.

pip install mpremote

items.title: 3. Find the serial port
items.text: Typical serial device names: - Linux:/dev/ttyACM0 or /dev/ttyUSB0 - macOS:/dev/cu.usbmodem... - Windows:COM3, COM4, etc. Test connection: mpremote connect auto fs ls If needed, specify manually: mpremote connect /dev/ttyACM0 fs ls

items.title: 4. Open the MicroPython REPL
items.text:
mpremote connect auto repl

Example: print("Hello from ESP32-C3")

Exit REPL with: Ctrl+]

items.title: 5. Upload a script
items.text: Example blink.py: mpremote connect auto fs cp blink.py :blink.py   The : means the device filesystem.

items.title: 6. Run a script without saving
items.text: mpremote connect auto run blink.py

items.title: 7. Run code automatically on boot
items.text:
MicroPython uses: - boot.py - main.py Upload: 

mpremote connect auto fs cp main.py :main.py
mpremote connect auto reset

items.title: 8. Upload a library folder
items.text:
mpremote connect auto fs mkdir :lib
mpremote connect auto fs cp -r lib/. :lib/

items.title: 9. Useful commands
items.text:
List files: mpremote connect auto fs ls

View file: mpremote connect auto fs cat :main.py   Delete file: mpremote connect auto fs rm :main.py   Reset board: mpremote connect auto reset

items.title: 10. Minimal example using the 5 on-board LEDs
items.text:
main.py:

from machine import Pin
from neopixel import NeoPixel
from time import sleep

NUM_LEDS = 5
PIN_NUM = 5

np = NeoPixel(Pin(PIN_NUM), NUM_LEDS)

while True:
for i in range(NUM_LEDS): # Red
np[i] = (20, 0, 0)
np.write()
sleep(0.5)
for i in range(NUM_LEDS): # Green
np[i] = (0, 20, 0)
np.write()
sleep(0.5)
for i in range(NUM_LEDS): # Blue
np[i] = (0, 0, 20)
np.write()
sleep(0.5)
for i in range(NUM_LEDS): # Off
np[i] = (0, 0, 0)
np.write()
sleep(0.5)

Upload: mpremote connect auto fs cp main.py :main.py mpremote connect auto reset

items.title: Board not detected
items.text:
• try another USB cable
  • reconnect the board
  • check the serial port name

items.title: Permission denied on Linux
items.text: sudo usermod -a -G dialout $USER Log out and log back in.

items.title: Code crashes on boot
items.text: Your main.py may contain an error. Replace or remove it using mpremote.
